title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read an article. For questions 31–36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you read.

content.title: The Intricacies of Memory: A Philosophical Perspective
content.subtitle: Exploring how we remember and why it matters
content.text: Memory, in its complexity, has long fascinated philosophers and scientists alike. The interplay between recollection and forgetfulness presents a paradox that [31]many have attempted to unravel[/31]. While some view memory as a mere repository of past experiences, others consider it a dynamic force that shapes our identity.||Philosopher John Locke famously argued that personal identity is contingent upon continuous memory. This proposition, while intuitive, encounters challenges when one considers cases of amnesia or false memories. [32]Locke’s theory falters in accounting for the reliability of memories[/32], posing a critical question about the essence of selfhood.||Moreover, advancements in neuroscience have provided insights that challenge philosophical notions. The discovery that memory can be both reconstructive and predictive [33]raises questions about the authenticity of recalled experiences[/33]. This has led to a reevaluation of earlier ideas, suggesting that memories are not static but rather malleable constructs.||In the context of language acquisition, memory plays a pivotal role. [34]The ability to retain and retrieve linguistic information[/34] forms the backbone of communication. Yet, the process by which we internalize vocabulary and grammar remains elusive. Some linguists argue that this is due to the brain’s inherent capacity to create neural patterns that mirror language rules, a theory that remains contentious among scholars.||Furthermore, in the realm of sport psychology, memory is instrumental in enhancing performance. Athletes often rely on muscle memory, a phenomenon that [35]reflects the mind’s power to internalize physical tasks[/35]. This type of memory bypasses conscious thought, allowing for seamless execution under pressure. However, the debate persists on whether muscle memory is purely physical or interwoven with cognitive elements.||Ultimately, the ethics of technology interfaces with memory in contemporary society. With digital devices augmenting our ability to store and recall information, [36]questions arise about cognitive dependence[/36]. While some praise this as a boon for productivity, others warn of the diminishing cognitive autonomy it may engender.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the writer suggests that memory...
options: A) is solely a biological function.
B) presents a challenge to understanding identity.
C) is less important than people believe.
D) is a universally accepted concept in philosophy.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31]many have attempted to unravel[/31], indicating the complexity and challenge. A is incorrect as there's no mention of it being purely biological. C contradicts the focus on its intricacy, and D is incorrect due to the ongoing debates.

questions.question: The mention of Locke's theory is used to...
options: A) dismiss all philosophical theories on memory.
B) highlight a theory's limitation in explaining identity.
C) show a universally accepted theory in philosophy.
D) introduce a new theory on memory.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [32]Locke’s theory falters[/32], pointing out its limitations. A is incorrect as the text focuses on one theory, not dismissing all. C is incorrect due to the text mentioning challenges, and D is irrelevant in context.

questions.question: What does the writer mean by 'authenticity of recalled experiences'?
options: A) Whether memories are accurate reflections of events.
B) The ability to enhance memory recall through technology.
C) The reliability of neuroscientific methods.
D) How memories influence creative processes.
questions.explanation: A is supported by [33]raises questions about the authenticity[/33], focusing on accuracy. B is incorrect as technology is not mentioned here, C is a misinterpretation, and D is irrelevant.

questions.question: The main purpose of the fourth paragraph is to...
options: A) discuss the role of memory in learning languages.
B) argue against language learning theories.
C) propose a new method of language teaching.
D) critique the use of digital tools in education.
questions.explanation: A is supported by [34]The ability to retain and retrieve linguistic information[/34], focusing on the role of memory in language. B is incorrect as there is no opposition, C and D are not the focus.

questions.question: In discussing muscle memory, the writer implies that...
options: A) it is entirely a mental process.
B) athletes rely too heavily on conscious thought.
C) it is a subject of debate regarding physical and cognitive aspects.
D) it is unrelated to performance enhancement.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [35]debate persists on whether muscle memory[/35], highlighting the intertwined aspects. A is not fully supported, B contradicts muscle memory's nature, and D is incorrect.

questions.question: The writer's attitude towards technology and memory can best be described as...
options: A) overwhelmingly positive.
B) cautiously critical.
C) dismissive of technological advances.
D) entirely negative.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [36]questions arise about cognitive dependence[/36], showing careful consideration. A is incorrect due to noted concerns, C and D are incorrect as the critique is not absolute.
